verses.text: Josiah was eight years old when he began to reign, and he reigned thirty and one years in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Jedidah, the daughter of Adaiah of Boscath. And he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, and walked in all the way of David his father, and turned not aside to the right hand or to the left. Guidance: Josiah — the last great king of Judah. Out of the wicked line of Manasseh and Amon comes Judah's final reformer. Josiah takes the throne at just eight years old and reigns thirty-one years. The verdict is among the highest in Kings: 'he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, and walked in all the way of David his father, and turned not aside to the right hand or to the left.' Like Hezekiah, he is measured against David himself; and the phrase 'turned not aside to the right hand or to the left' echoes the LORD's charge to Joshua and the ideal of the king in Deuteronomy 17:20 — perfect, undeviating obedience to the law. The chapter is showing us, once more, the LORD's grace breaking into the darkest dynastic decline. Just as Hezekiah came from the apostate Ahaz, Josiah comes from the catastrophic Manasseh and the godless Amon. A boy raised in a court still reeking of Manasseh's idolatry and his father Amon's murder grows up to be the most thoroughly obedient king the nation ever had. The chapter is teaching us that no family line, however corrupt, can prevent the LORD from raising up a devoted servant; grace is not bound by heredity. The chapter is also teaching us the beauty of a faith formed young — Josiah began to seek the LORD as a boy (2 Chr 34:3 says he began seeking God in the eighth year of his reign, at about sixteen), and his lifelong, undeviating obedience flowed from an early and wholehearted turning. The chapter is preparing us for the discovery that will define his reign — the rediscovery of the Book of the Law — and for the most sweeping reformation in Judah's history, all flowing from a king who 'turned not aside to the right hand or to the left.'

And it came to pass in the eighteenth year of king Josiah, that the king sent Shaphan the son of Azaliah, the son of Meshullam, the scribe, to the house of the LORD, saying, Go up to Hilkiah the high priest, that he may sum the silver which is brought into the house of the LORD, which the keepers of the door have gathered of the people: And let them deliver it into the hand of the doers of the work, that have the oversight of the house of the LORD: and let them give it to the doers of the work which is in the house of the LORD, to repair the breaches of the house, Unto carpenters, and builders, and masons, and to buy timber and hewn stone to repair the house. Howbeit there was no reckoning made with them of the money that was delivered into their hand, because they dealt faithfully. Guidance: The temple repair — and faithful workmen again. In his eighteenth year, Josiah commissions the repair of the temple, which had fallen into disrepair under the long apostasy of Manasseh and Amon. The arrangement closely follows the model Joash had established (12:9-15): the door-keeping priests gather the people's offerings, the high priest Hilkiah sums it, and it is given to the foremen who pay the carpenters, builders, and masons and buy timber and stone. And once more the narrator notes the integrity of those handling the funds: 'there was no reckoning made with them of the money... because they dealt faithfully.' The chapter is showing us a king restoring the LORD's house — the visible center of true worship, neglected and defiled through decades of idolatry. As with Joash and Hezekiah, the care of the temple is a mark of a reforming king. The chapter is teaching us, in the repeated note about faithful workmen ('they dealt faithfully'), the same lesson as in Joash's day: the highest aim of accountability is to produce, and to be able to trust, people of genuine integrity — where faithfulness is proven, oversight can be light. But the chapter is also setting the stage for something far greater than a building repair. In the very process of cleaning out and restoring the long-neglected temple, a discovery is about to be made that will shake the king to his core and ignite the greatest reformation in the nation's history. The chapter is teaching us that faithful, ordinary obedience — simply attending to the upkeep of the LORD's house — can become the occasion for an extraordinary work of God: the rediscovery of His word.

And Hilkiah the high priest said unto Shaphan the scribe, I have found the book of the law in the house of the LORD. And Hilkiah gave the book to Shaphan, and he read it. And Shaphan the scribe came to the king, and brought the king word again, and said, Thy servants have gathered the money that was found in the house, and have delivered it into the hand of them that do the work, that have the oversight of the house of the LORD. And Shaphan the scribe shewed the king, saying, Hilkiah the priest hath delivered me a book. And Shaphan read it before the king. Guidance: The Book of the Law rediscovered. In the midst of the repairs, the high priest Hilkiah makes a momentous discovery: 'I have found the book of the law in the house of the LORD.' (This was almost certainly a copy of Deuteronomy, or the Torah, that had been lost or hidden during the long decades of Manasseh's idolatry — a stunning measure of how far Judah had fallen, that the very law of God had to be FOUND, like buried treasure, in His own house.) Hilkiah gives it to Shaphan the scribe, who reads it himself and then brings it to the king. Shaphan first reports on the repair-money (the ordinary business), then almost as an afterthought mentions, 'Hilkiah the priest hath delivered me a book' — and reads it before the king. The chapter is showing us the tragic backdrop and the gracious turning-point together. The tragic backdrop: God's word had been so neglected that it was effectively lost — a nation that had forgotten the Scriptures it was founded on. The gracious turning-point: in the very act of restoring the temple, the word was found and brought before a king whose heart was ready to receive it. The chapter is teaching us the irreplaceable centrality of God's written word. Everything that follows — the king's repentance, the great covenant renewal, the sweeping reformation, the Passover — all of it flows from the reading of the rediscovered book. Reformation began not with the king's good intentions alone but with the recovery and hearing of Scripture. The chapter is teaching us that when God's word is neglected, a people drift into darkness, but when it is recovered and read, it has power to ignite repentance and renewal. The chapter is also quietly profound: the law had been lost in the temple itself — religion can continue its outward forms (offerings, repairs, priests) while the actual word of God lies forgotten in a corner. The recovery of the book is the recovery of the standard against which everything must be measured.

And it came to pass, when the king had heard the words of the book of the law, that he rent his clothes. And the king commanded Hilkiah the priest, and Ahikam the son of Shaphan, and Achbor the son of Michaiah, and Shaphan the scribe, and Asahiah a servant of the king's, saying, Go ye, enquire of the LORD for me, and for the people, and for all Judah, concerning the words of this book that is found: for great is the wrath of the LORD that is kindled against us, because our fathers have not hearkened unto the words of this book, to do according unto all that which is written concerning us. Guidance: The word pierces the king's heart. The reaction of Josiah to hearing God's word is the mark of a truly tender heart: 'when the king had heard the words of the book of the law, that he rent his clothes.' He is cut to the heart — for the book (Deuteronomy) contained the covenant curses, the warnings of exile and desolation for the very idolatry Judah had practiced for generations, and Josiah recognized at once how far the nation had fallen and how great the danger. His response is not defensiveness but grief and immediate action: he sends a delegation — the high priest, the scribe, and three senior officials — to 'enquire of the LORD for me, and for the people, and for all Judah.' His reasoning shows genuine fear of God: 'great is the wrath of the LORD that is kindled against us, because our fathers have not hearkened unto the words of this book.' The chapter is showing us the right response to encountering God's word: to let it expose us, humble us, and drive us to seek the LORD. Josiah did not explain the warnings away, minimize them, or assume they applied to someone else; he took them personally and trembled. The chapter is teaching us what a 'tender heart' (v19) looks like — a heart so responsive to God's word that the warnings of Scripture produce genuine conviction and immediate action rather than indifference or excuse-making. The chapter is teaching us the difference between merely HEARING the word and being PIERCED by it. Many had access to the law over the centuries; Josiah heard it and tore his clothes. The chapter is teaching us that God's word does its deepest work in the heart that is soft enough to be wounded by it — and that such a wounding, far from being destructive, is the doorway to repentance, reformation, and the mercy the next verses will reveal. The king who trembles at the word is the king the LORD will commend.

So Hilkiah the priest, and Ahikam, and Achbor, and Shaphan, and Asahiah, went unto Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the son of Tikvah, the son of Harhas, keeper of the wardrobe; (now she dwelt in Jerusalem in the college;) and they communed with her. And she said unto them, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Tell the man that sent you to me, Thus saith the LORD, Behold, I will bring evil upon this place, and upon the inhabitants thereof, even all the words of the book which the king of Judah hath read: Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the works of their hands; therefore my wrath shall be kindled against this place, and shall not be quenched. Guidance: Huldah the prophetess confirms the judgment. The royal delegation goes to inquire of the LORD through Huldah, a prophetess living in Jerusalem (a notable detail — she ministered in the same era as Jeremiah and Zephaniah, yet it was to this woman prophet that the high priest and the king's officials came; the LORD speaks through whom He chooses). Her word is unflinching. First, the verdict on the nation: 'I will bring evil upon this place, and upon the inhabitants thereof, even all the words of the book which the king of Judah hath read.' Every curse Josiah heard and trembled at will come to pass. The reason: 'Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods... therefore my wrath... shall not be quenched.' The judgment on Judah, decreed because of Manasseh's provocations (21:10-15), is confirmed: the exile will come; the reformation, however great, will not avert it. The chapter is showing us that even genuine, wholehearted reformation under a godly king does not automatically cancel a judgment the nation has earned. Huldah does not soften the verdict to please the king; she speaks the LORD's true word. The chapter is teaching us the integrity of authentic prophecy: Huldah told the king's delegation what the LORD said, not what they might have hoped to hear. The chapter is teaching us a sobering truth about national sin: the accumulated provocations of generations can reach a point where the consequences must fall, even when a faithful remnant and a godly king arise. But the chapter is also setting up the personal mercy that follows: the same God who will not quench His wrath against the nation has a tender, particular word for the king whose heart was soft. The chapter is teaching us that God's word distinguishes between the unrepentant nation and the humbled individual — judgment on the one, mercy on the other — as the next verses will movingly show.

So Hilkiah the priest, and Ahikam, and Achbor, and Shaphan, and Asahiah, went unto Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the son of Tikvah, the son of Harhas, keeper of the wardrobe; (now she dwelt in Jerusalem in the college;) and they communed with her. And she said unto them, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Tell the man that sent you to me, Thus saith the LORD, Behold, I will bring evil upon this place, and upon the inhabitants thereof, even all the words of the book which the king of Judah hath read: Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the works of their hands; therefore my wrath shall be kindled against this place, and shall not be quenched. Guidance: Huldah the prophetess confirms the judgment. The royal delegation goes to inquire of the LORD through Huldah, a prophetess living in Jerusalem (a notable detail — she ministered in the same era as Jeremiah and Zephaniah, yet it was to this woman prophet that the high priest and the king's officials came; the LORD speaks through whom He chooses). Her word is unflinching. First, the verdict on the nation: 'I will bring evil upon this place, and upon the inhabitants thereof, even all the words of the book which the king of Judah hath read.' Every curse Josiah heard and trembled at will come to pass. The reason: 'Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods... therefore my wrath... shall not be quenched.' The judgment on Judah, decreed because of Manasseh's provocations (21:10-15), is confirmed: the exile will come; the reformation, however great, will not avert it. The chapter is showing us that even genuine, wholehearted reformation under a godly king does not automatically cancel a judgment the nation has earned. Huldah does not soften the verdict to please the king; she speaks the LORD's true word. The chapter is teaching us the integrity of authentic prophecy: Huldah told the king's delegation what the LORD said, not what they might have hoped to hear. The chapter is teaching us a sobering truth about national sin: the accumulated provocations of generations can reach a point where the consequences must fall, even when a faithful remnant and a godly king arise. But the chapter is also setting up the personal mercy that follows: the same God who will not quench His wrath against the nation has a tender, particular word for the king whose heart was soft. The chapter is teaching us that God's word distinguishes between the unrepentant nation and the humbled individual — judgment on the one, mercy on the other — as the next verses will movingly show.

So Hilkiah the priest, and Ahikam, and Achbor, and Shaphan, and Asahiah, went unto Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the son of Tikvah, the son of Harhas, keeper of the wardrobe; (now she dwelt in Jerusalem in the college;) and they communed with her. And she said unto them, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Tell the man that sent you to me, Thus saith the LORD, Behold, I will bring evil upon this place, and upon the inhabitants thereof, even all the words of the book which the king of Judah hath read: Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the works of their hands; therefore my wrath shall be kindled against this place, and shall not be quenched. Guidance: Huldah the prophetess confirms the judgment. Every curse Josiah heard and trembled at will come to pass. The reason: 'Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods... therefore my wrath... shall not be quenched.' The judgment on Judah, decreed because of Manasseh's provocations (21:10-15), is confirmed: the exile will come; the reformation, however great, will not avert it. The chapter is showing us that even genuine, wholehearted reformation under a godly king does not automatically cancel a judgment the nation has earned. Huldah does not soften the verdict to please the king; she speaks the LORD's true word. The chapter is teaching us the integrity of authentic prophecy: Huldah told the king's delegation what the LORD said, not what they might have hoped to hear. The chapter is teaching us a sobering truth about national sin: the accumulated provocations of generations can reach a point where the consequences must fall, even when a faithful remnant and a godly king arise. But the chapter is also setting up the personal mercy that follows: the same God who will not quench His wrath against the nation has a tender, particular word for the king whose heart was soft. The chapter is teaching us that God's word distinguishes between the unrepentant nation and the humbled individual — judgment on the one, mercy on the other — as the next verses will movingly show.

But to the king of Judah which sent you to enquire of the LORD, thus shall ye say to him, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, As touching the words which thou hast heard; Because thine heart was tender, and thou hast humbled thyself before the LORD, when thou heardest what I spake against this place, and against the inhabitants thereof, that they should become a desolation and a curse, and hast rent thy clothes, and wept before me; I also have heard thee, saith the LORD. Behold therefore, I will gather thee unto thy fathers, and thou shalt be gathered into thy grave in peace; and thine eyes shall not see all the evil which I will bring upon this place. And they brought the king word again. Guidance: Mercy for the tender heart. Huldah's word turns from the nation to the king personally, and here is pure grace. 'Because thine heart was tender, and thou hast humbled thyself before the LORD... and hast rent thy clothes, and wept before me; I also have heard thee, saith the LORD.' The LORD takes specific notice of Josiah's response — his tender heart, his humility, his torn clothes, his tears — and answers it with personal mercy: 'I will gather thee unto thy fathers, and thou shalt be gathered into thy grave in peace; and thine eyes shall not see all the evil which I will bring upon this place.' Josiah will die before the judgment falls; he will be spared the sight of Jerusalem's destruction. (His later death in battle at Megiddo, v29-30, is 'in peace' in the deeper sense — he died and was buried honorably in his own land before the catastrophe of exile, not amid it.) The chapter is showing us the LORD's tender attentiveness to a humble, responsive heart even in a time of national doom. The judgment on Judah stands, but the LORD singles out the one who trembled at His word and grants him personal mercy. The chapter is teaching us the immense value the LORD places on a tender, humble heart: 'thine heart was tender, and thou hast humbled thyself... and wept before me; I also have heard thee.' God notices and honors genuine contrition. The chapter is teaching us that even when we cannot avert every consequence of a long-accumulated sin (our own or our community's), humbling ourselves before God is never wasted — He hears, He sees the tears, and He responds with mercy suited to the humbled heart. The chapter is teaching us that the right response to God's warnings is exactly Josiah's response — a tender heart, humility, grief over sin — and that such a response, while it may not always change the outward course of events, always finds a gracious hearing with the LORD. The faithful king who could not save his nation was himself granted peace; God's mercy to the humble is personal and sure.
